rule: Everything in the course is reversible — any passage can be read backwards and still make musical sense.
The higher you go, the freer you get — register relaxes the rules.
There is no key. All Root tones are of equal importance. A tonal center can exist only if you want one.
The bar line is the integrating force. Inside a single bar everything shares one Root, scale, chord, and NCT set.
The method requires only about half a dozen rules plus practice.
EIS is derived from the overtone series, not from Schoenberg, Slonimsky, or Schillinger.
Harmony is a set of tones from one scale sounded simultaneously. All chords are built on intervals belonging to the scales.
Use a Two-Octave Scale when building chords.
Vertical spacing follows the overtone series as closely as possible.
Mark everything — it will come in handy for reference later.
Step 1: begin with Root tones (pick an E-cycle or mixture).
Step 2: choose the scales over each Root (from the 18).
Step 3: build harmonies vertically from the chosen scale.
Step 4: finalize via VL, NCTs, resolutions, and reverse resolutions.
Three Basic Principles to master: Equal-interval Root tones; the 18 scales; the vertical chord structures built from those scales.
Overall goal: weave melodic and harmonic lines through the three principles.
A melody may be a single line with harmony in the bass clef, or 2P-H3, 3P, 4P, 5P, or complete chords.
Root progressions are built from 6 equal-interval cycles (E1–E6).
E1 = minor 2nd (semitone); cycle length 12.
E2 = major 2nd (whole tone); cycle length 6.
E3 = minor 3rd; cycle length 4.
E4 = major 3rd; cycle length 3.
E5 = perfect 4th; cycle length 12 (circle of 4ths).
E6 = tritone; cycle length 2.
The perfect 5th is treated as an octave position of a 4th — there is no separate E7.
Successive Root tones in sequence are called a Progression; 'progression' refers only to horizontal movement of the bass.
Elision: leave out any bass intervals not needed to the pattern or not conforming to the meter in the treble.
In E5, E4, E3, and E6 the bass notes may appear in any order of appearance between Root tones.
Ascending E-progression: the last tone in the bar is the Root tone.
The ascending form of a progression is the exact opposite of the descending form (reversibility).
Hold common tones when moving to the next chord.
Move remaining tones to the nearest tones in the new chord.
Use contrary motion between one or more parts and the bass.
Do not write three tones together, regardless of octave voicing.
Non-chord tones do not affect voice-leading; they are placed between two chord tones.
In chords containing natural 7, the ♮7 must always resolve (to 6) whenever 9 resolves to 8.
Change-of-Position (C.O.P.) of the treble uses voice-leading when crossing the bar line.
Use Substitute Position (S.P.) when no tone is held over in the same voice from one chord to the next.
Dominant-7 VL: 3 → ♭7 and ♭7 → 3 with 1 → 1.
Dominant-7 VL: 3 → ♭7 and ♭7 → 3 with 5 → 5.
Dominant-7 VL: same-bar R → 5 with brackets [ ] on 3, ♭7.
Parallel voice-leading is allowed as a named variant.
Bracket VL [ ]: one tone will always be missing.
Watch for parallel octaves between the bass and any note in the treble.
Substitute Voice-Leading (S.V.L.) is an alternative move available alongside VL and S.P.
No harmony tone is written below the 3rd space of the bass clef.
exceptions: lowest Root or 5th may go lower

rule: The range limit may be extended when the bass is in motion (e.g. a bass solo).
Generally avoid Outside-Octave Dissonance (OOD).
♭9 is OK if ♭7 is in the chord.
♭2 is OK over a pedal.
3 & 11 together are not good.
4 & 10 together are fine.
11 & 17 follow the same pattern as 3 & 11 — not good.
There are six types of NCT: P.T., C.A., R.T., C.T., Sus., Ant.
P.T. = Passing Tone.
C.A. = Chromatic Alteration.
R.T. = Returning Tone (neighbor).
C.T. = Chord Tone used as a passing or substitute chord tone (e.g. in bass).
Sus. = Suspension.
Ant. = Anticipation.
Substitute Tones (S.T.) can replace chord tones: 6 for 5, 4 for 3, 2 for 1, 9 for 3.
When a Substitute Tone appears first and resolves back to the chord tone it is a Leading Tone.
When the chord tone appears first and the Substitute Tone follows, the S.T. is a Passing Tone.
NCTs substitute for or alter the chord tones.
NCTs may be used in combinations or singly.
Bass may move ascending or descending.
Bass motion modes: regular, free, elision, passing 3rds, passing 5ths, leading tones.
Passing 3rds: the Root tone must appear first.
Passing 5ths: no required ordering.
When a Passing 3rd sounds metrically with the treble structure, double the Root or 5th in the treble and omit the 3rd.
Passing 3rds & 5ths may be combined with Equal Intervals in the bass.
Chord-tone and Equal-Interval bass may combine with Leading Tones and Passing Tones (2→1, 4→3, 1→2, 3→4).
Any bass tone may be repeated an octave higher or lower (e.g. T 8-3 5).
Descending bass: use diatonic motion on p.5 (E7), EIS on all others.
Bass and treble motion may be combined — bass moves and treble changes position simultaneously.
The Six-Four is a separate chord with Root in the bass.
Standard Six-Four resolution: 6→5, 4→3 (or the reverse).
In minor, Six-Four resolution may use either 5 or 6.
6 and 4 in the Six-Four are treated as Substitute Tones.
Every chord class has its own resolutions and reverse resolutions.
Scales may be mixed across a resolution (e.g. Scale #8 → Scale #9).
6 may be substituted for 7 in all 9th, 11th, and 13th chords.
There are 18 scales, each based on the Natural Major scale but with diatonic intervals.
Good 4th-chord scales: 1, 3, 4, 7 (and 11, 12 in 4-part); possibly 4+.
Scale #10 generates Dominant 7♭9.
Overtone-series 1st octave: fundamental and its octave.
Overtone-series 2nd octave: Root and 5th.
Overtone-series 3rd octave: 1, 3, 5, ♭7 — the Chord Tones.
Overtone-series 4th octave: 1, 2, 3, 4+, 5, 6, ♭7 — Scale Tones.
Overtone-series 5th octave: 1, 2, 3, 4+, 5, 6, ♭7 — Scale Tones.
Triads (major & minor): close & open voicings, 3 inversions each, with VL / S.V.L. / S.P.
Natural 7ths (major & minor): all inversions close/open, resolutions & reverse, C.O.P., NCTs, bass-in-motion, substitute tones (9 for 3).
4P Natural 9ths (Δ9), m♮9, 6th, m6th: all inversions, resolutions to 7th or 6th chords (and reverse), C.O.P., NCTs; VL ↑, ↓, brackets or parallel.
Dominant 7ths: 2P / 3P / 4P. VL variants: normal, brackets, 3↔♭7 with 1→1, 3↔♭7 with 5→5, same-bar R→5 with [ ] on 3,♭7, parallel, S.V.L./S.P.
Minor 7ths: all VL variants above; also m7♭5, Δ7+, 7♭5; 2P Δ7, mΔ7, 6, m6.
Minor 9ths: 3P and 4P; same treatment as minor 7ths.
Dominant 7♭9: use Scale #10. All VL variants above apply.
Dominant 9ths: 2P / 3P / 4P. Different 3P voicings → different tones may be added to bass in parallel.
Dominant 13ths constraint: only ♭10 with 11, or 10 with 11+; or 11 for 10.
Dominant 11 and Dominant 11+: 3P and 4P; or 11th from Scale #9 or #10; parallel bass options. For 11+ also 3 in parallel with the bass.
5-part chords are referenced but not fully detailed in Greene's pages.
The advanced course introduces quartal (4th-based) harmony.
The advanced course introduces quintal (5th-based) harmony.
The advanced course introduces secondal (2nd-based) harmony.
The advanced course introduces polytonality.
The advanced course introduces interval relations across parts.
The advanced course introduces music with no chords and no scales.
The advanced course introduces 'tropes' — Murphy's version of serialism.
Elementary rules (range, OOD, etc.) progressively relax as the student becomes freer in later books.
Triad progressions in all ascending/descending symmetric cycles (close, open, and switching between them) are a first-class composing resource.
Use NCTs in combinations or singly as a composing resource.
Use Substitute Tones as a composing resource.
Six-Four chords with their resolutions and reverse resolutions are a composing resource.
Treble C.O.P. (with or without S.P.) across the bar line is a composing resource.
Bass in Motion (ascending/descending; regular/free/elision/passing 3rds/5ths/leading tones) is a composing resource.
Bass and treble motion combined is a composing resource.
C.O.P. of any one chord connection or progression (ascending, descending, mixed) is a composing resource.
Many of the writing resources can and should be combined.
